title: Debugging Basics
description: Learn to read errors, find bugs, and fix them systematically.
objectives: Identify common error types (SyntaxError, TypeError, NameError, IndexError)
Use print() to debug step by step
recap: Reading error messages carefully is the #1 debugging skill. Python tells you the error type, the line number, and often what went wrong. Print statements help you inspect values at runtime.
practicePrompts: Intentionally cause a TypeError and then fix it based on the error message.
Add print() statements to trace a bug in a loop that produces wrong output.

practiceExercises.title: Fix the TypeError
practiceExercises.prompt: The starter code has a TypeError — it tries to concatenate a string with an int. Fix it so the program prints exactly `You are 25 years old`.
practiceExercises.goal: Read the traceback, identify the type mismatch, and fix it.
practiceExercises.starterCode:
age = 25
print("You are " + age + " years old")
hints: Strings and ints can't be concatenated with `+`.
Or use an f-string: `f"You are {age} years old"`.

practiceExercises.title: Fix the IndexError
practiceExercises.prompt:
The starter loops past the end of the list, causing an IndexError. Fix it so the program prints every item, one per line.
Expected output:
apple
banana
cherry
practiceExercises.goal: Spot an off-by-one loop bound and fix it.
hints: Or even simpler: `for fruit in fruits:` skips indices entirely.

practiceExercises.title: Fix the logic bug
practiceExercises.prompt: This function is supposed to return the SUM of a list, but it's returning the wrong value. Use print() to trace the bug, then fix it. Expected output: `Sum is 15`.
practiceExercises.goal: Use print-debugging to find a loop accumulator bug.
hints: Add `print(s)` inside the loop to see how s changes.
`s = n` overwrites the total every time — you want `s += n`.
